nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, floodplains, swamps, and muddy-bottomed lakes; prefers soft substrates where it can burrow; found in lowland areas with abundant leaf litter and submerged vegetation

decorPreferences: deep soft sand substrate (minimum 10 cm) for burrowing
large smooth stones and cave-like shelters
driftwood and bogwood for hiding spots
PVC pipe sections as artificial burrows
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity
no sharp gravel or coarse substrate (damages scaleless-like skin)

diet.notes: Predatory carnivore; in the wild feeds primarily on small fish, worms, crustaceans, and insect larvae. In captivity, readily accepts earthworms, bloodworms (fresh, frozen, or freeze-dried), prawns, mussel, and other meaty foods. Can usually be trained to accept sinking pellets and carnivore sticks over time. Feeds predominantly at night; target-feeding with tongs is often necessary to ensure the eel receives food in a community tank. Avoid feeder fish as a dietary staple due to disease risk and nutritional imbalance.

goodWith: Bichirs and Ropefish of similar size
other robust fish too large to be swallowed
avoidWith: aggressive cichlids that may harass the eel
other spiny eels unless tank is very large with multiple hiding spots

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Mastacembelus erythrotaenia; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: The Fire Eel is the largest commonly kept spiny eel, reaching 60–100 cm in aquariums (up to 1 m in the wild). Native to slow rivers and floodplains of Southeast Asia, it demands a soft sand substrate, is strictly nocturnal, and is a confirmed piscivore. Renowned for its intelligence — individuals often recognise their keeper and can be hand-fed.
careNotes:
The Fire Eel (Mastacembelus erythrotaenia) is the most impressive of the spiny eels kept in the hobby, attaining 60–80 cm under aquarium conditions and occasionally approaching 1 m in very large systems. A tank of at least 400 L with a 180 cm or greater footprint is the minimum for an adult; longer is strongly preferred. The substrate must be deep, fine-grained soft sand (minimum 10 cm); this species has delicate scaleless-like skin that is easily abraded by gravel or coarse substrates, leading to wounds and secondary infection. All hard decor should be smooth-edged.

The Fire Eel is strictly nocturnal and burrowing — it spends much of the day buried in substrate or wedged in a cave, emerging at dusk to hunt. Feeding is best done after lights-out using tongs or a feeding stick; in community tanks it can be easily outcompeted for food. Earthworms and high-quality frozen bloodworms are excellent staple foods; sinking carnivore pellets can be introduced gradually. Live feeder fish should be avoided as a primary diet.

Like all spiny eels, the Fire Eel is highly copper-sensitive; all copper-based medications must be avoided, and even trace copper from pipes or décor can be harmful. The species is a notorious escape artist — rated high escape risk — and all tank openings, including filter inlets and any small gap in the hood, must be sealed completely. Even a small gap is sufficient for this powerful, flexible fish to squeeze through and escape.

Fire Eels are piscivores: any fish small enough to fit in their mouth will eventually be eaten. Plan tankmates accordingly — keep only fish larger than roughly one-third the eel’s body length. Despite their predatory nature, Fire Eels are noted for high intelligence among aquarium fish; experienced keepers report individuals recognising specific people, accepting hand-feeding of worms, and exhibiting distinct personalities. Water quality must be excellent: they are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite, and elevated nitrates lead to skin problems. Weekly large water changes (30–40%) are recommended. Maintain temperature 24–28°C and slightly soft, mildly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 5–15).

Distinguish the Fire Eel from the similar Tire Track Eel (M. armatus): the Tire Track has a banded/reticulated pattern along the lateral line, distinct dark flagging on the dorsal spines, and stays somewhat smaller. The Fire Eel shows bold red-orange lateral stripes and spots on a dark brown body. Smaller spiny eels in the genus Macrognathus (e.g., Half-Banded Spiny Eel, Peacock Spiny Eel) are a separate genus and remain much smaller (15–30 cm).
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare and has only been reported sporadically. Attempts typically require a large species-only tank, conditioning with live foods, a seasonal temperature drop followed by a warm-up to simulate monsoon onset, and thick vegetation or moss for egg deposition. Eggs and fry are rarely raised successfully in home aquariums. The species is not bred commercially; virtually all trade specimens are wild-caught.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Mastacembelus erythrotaenia
primary.notes: Primary source for taxonomy, water parameters (24–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm), adult size, native range, diet, and husbandry requirements including substrate and escape risk.

additional.name: FishBase — Mastacembelus erythrotaenia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 100 cm TL), native range (Mekong, Chao Phraya, and Malay Peninsula drainages), taxonomy (Mastacembelidae, Synbranchiformes), and temperature tolerance. Wild maximum 1 m confirmed.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Spiny Eel Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide covering Fire Eel husbandry: sand substrate requirement, copper sensitivity, escape risk (tight-fitting lid essential), nocturnal feeding habits, and temperament notes. Tank size recommendations (125+ gallon for large species) consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Fire Eel profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist source confirming aquarium size 60–80 cm typical, care level advanced, soft sand substrate necessity, nocturnal behaviour, piscivore diet, copper sensitivity, and the species’ reputation for intelligence and keeper recognition.

additional.name: Seriously Fish — Mastacembelidae family overview
additional.notes: Family-level context: distribution across South and Southeast Asia, burrowing behaviour common to all Mastacembelus, scaleless skin vulnerability, and copper sensitivity across the family.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is extremely rare; wild-caught specimens dominate the trade. Occasional reports of egg-scattering in very large tanks after seasonal conditioning, but successful fry rearing at home is virtually undocumented.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 60–80 cm in aquaria (1 m wild); needs 500 L+ tank with deep sand and sealed gaps. Specialist commitment.
